name: Baby car seats
summary: Infant, convertible, and booster car seats for children — safety-critical category with federal regulation.

evidence.claim: NHTSA FMVSS 213 is the federal safety standard for child restraint systems. All car seats sold in the US must meet it; no seat is 'safer' than another on the base standard because all pass the same test.

NHTSA Ease-of-Use ratings (1-5 stars) rate how straightforward the seat is to install and use correctly; seats with high Ease-of-Use ratings correlate with reduced misuse rates in real-world installation.
Car seat expiration dates are manufacturer-stated, typically 6-10 years from date of manufacture. Material degradation (plastic, foam) is the primary concern.

criteria.name: nhtsa_fmvss_213_certified
criteria.notes: All US-sold seats must meet this (E1). Required, not differentiator.

criteria.name: nhtsa_ease_of_use_rating
criteria.notes: Lower rating correlates with installation errors (E2).

confabulationPatterns.pattern: safest car seat on the market
confabulationPatterns.reality: All US-sold car seats meet the same federal safety standard FMVSS 213 (E1). 'Safest' is marketing; correct installation matters far more than brand.
confabulationPatterns.checkPrompt: Is the 'safest' claim framed as surpassing FMVSS 213? No US seat legally sold is less safe than another on the base standard.

confabulationPatterns.reality: European standards are different from US FMVSS 213 but are not inherently superior; each is valid in its jurisdiction. A US-imported ECE-only seat is NOT legal for US highway use.
confabulationPatterns.checkPrompt: Is the seat FMVSS 213 certified for US use, or only ECE certified?

confabulationPatterns.reality: Car seats expire 6-10 years from manufacture date (E3). Longer 'usable years' in marketing does not mean longer-lasting materials; it may just mean the seat is a convertible / all-in-one.
confabulationPatterns.checkPrompt: Is the 'longest' claim about expiration, about weight-range coverage, or about general durability?

counterfeitSignals.signal: Car seat from marketplace seller below retailer price by >25%
counterfeitSignals.action: flag — counterfeit seats lack crash certification and are actively dangerous

counterfeitSignals.signal: Used car seat from private seller with unknown history
counterfeitSignals.action: flag — unknown crash history invalidates safety

compatibilityQuestions: Child's current weight and height — determines appropriate seat type.
Vehicle make/model — some car seats don't fit certain vehicles. Check manufacturer's vehicle-fit list.
LATCH or seatbelt install — LATCH has weight limits and may not work for all seat/car combos.

typicalHiddenCosts.name: Replacement after moderate-or-severe crash (insurance often covers)
Upgrade seats as child grows (3+ seats typical birth-to-12)

repairability.notes: Do not use aftermarket parts — invalidates FMVSS certification.
